Author: Bala jee
Title: Inside the mind of AI: How prompts really work
Text:
Inside the mind of AI: How prompts really work
These days, everyone is talking about prompt engineering, but have you stopped and thought about how a prompt actually makes AI perform a task? What background work happens before the response is generated? How does it understand what you actually want to ask?
And have you ever noticed that even when you type something wrong or make multiple mistakes, it still gives an accurate response?
Does this ever drive you crazy? How does it understand your context? If yes, congratulations to you. You are already doing great.
If not, we need to understand the end-to-end background work to understand how AI works, and moreover, how a good prompt plays a major role in prompt engineering, and also how a good prompt enables AI to perform at its best.
So, let’s understand the process with a bit of technical terms, ok, before diving deeper, let’s see the overview of how AI works upon any prompt:
So the whole process starts with the prompt creation, and the prompt can be anything created by the end user, and then the model comes up with an extraordinary response to the user’s requested API, let’s understand this with the help of the working diagram given below:
Now let’s dive deeper into each term:
1. Prompt creation:
Every-thing which you are feeding to ai to get the response is comes under the prompt, and when I said everything means every thing either it’s in the text format or its in the form of image its comes under the prompt and along with that we are also feeding information to the ai, or also we can say that we are training ai, so with this statement we can say that the ai models which we are using these days is learning model not the expert models, and with this I didn’t mean to say that the models available has lack of information but need to learn how to portray the information in more professional or may in the same tone to the user given prompt, we are the users who are training the models, have you ever noticed that whenever you u suggest the ai to talk in the same way how i’m talking to you, its try to match your way of talking, this is it for prompt now after writing prompt when u hit enter let’s see the next step of process.
2. Tokenization:
Before explaining this, we must be clear that a model cannot understand words, so here, tokenisation enters, which converts text into tokens.
For example:- explain the area of a square? (this gets converted into the [4821, 1203, 7843, 57, 4356])
It uses Byte Pair Encoding(BPE) or a similar tokeniser.
It splits words into subwords.
It maps each token to an integer ID.
So now the given prompt is being converted in the sequence of numbers, and this whole process comes under the tokenisation. Now, move ahead to see how this sequence of numbers helps the model to generate the output.
3. Embedding layer:
now the each token ID is converted into high dimensional vector. But here the question arises, why in the vector because when we are dealing with neural networks its very important that the data reaches the neural network in the vector format, because neural networks operate in vector space.
for example:- 4821->[0.021, -0.91, 0.044,…..4096 dimensions]
So now if there are 20 tokens and a 4096 embedding size, then the matrix= 20*4096
and above size of the matrix above is directly proportional to the type of content being generated by the model.
4. Positional encoding:
Now we have the data in the form of vector format now for providing the positional identity to each vector comes under the positional encoding. here each vector is provided a index number which helps the model to understand which word comes first and which one after.
Ex:-explain the area of a square? [(1)4821, (2)1203, (3)7843, (4)57, (5)4356]
now in the above example the number inside the parentheses is giving positional support to each generated token to help model understand which is coming before and after, and this plays very important role in order to generate the correct response.
now the positional encoding is being done let’s move to the next step.
5. Transformer processing:
A more advanced architecture that replaced RNNs for sequence modelling.(Recurrent neural network(RNNs) is a part of neural network which is being designed for sequential processing of data or we can say that in the linear form, so when the data is being processed in the sequential form the model don’t have the data of upcoming data, so for overcoming of this issue transformer comes into the field, transformer process the data parallelly which helps the model to understand the upcoming data in the given data, this helps to understand the meaning of particular word used in any sentence.)
A transformer basically helps the model to understand the exact meaning of words in sentence or even the context of sentence.
Ex:- He leaves the office early
So here leaves has different meaning at different places and the correct meaning of leaves is defined by transformer, it doesn’t store fixed meaning of any words, it computes the meaning based on context or the place of used.
6. Sampling strategy:
Now this is the most important part which will help you understand that even after giving the same prompt why we are getting the different responses. Let’s dive into it:
Imagine the model just finished reading your prompt. Now it’s standing at a crossroads.
In front of it are thousands of possible next words. For each word, it already knows the probability — like a scoreboard showing which word is most likely to come next.
Now comes the interesting part.
Sampling strategy is simply how the model decides which word to pick from that scoreboard.
If it always chooses the top word — the one with the highest probability — that’s called greedy decoding. It’s safe. It’s accurate. But after some time, it starts sounding robotic and repetitive.
Now enter temperature.
Think of temperature like a creativity knob.
Low temperature? The model plays safe. It sticks to the most obvious words.
High temperature? It becomes bold. It takes risks. Sometimes creative, sometimes unpredictable.
Then we have Top-K.
Here the model says, “Okay, I’ll only consider the top few strongest options.” It ignores the rest and chooses from that smaller pool.
And Top-P is even smarter. Instead of fixing a number, it gathers the smallest group of words that together make up most of the probability — and picks from there. It adapts based on the situation.
The important thing?
Sampling doesn’t change what the model knows.
It just changes how confidently or creatively it speaks.
Same brain=Different style of choosing words.
7. Decoding:
In last after all the process the model generated response which is in the form of numbers is being converted into the text format for the user under standing.
8. Response back to API:
The generated response is being sent to the same API from where the model is requested for the response, and here the process ends.
And congrats to you now you know everything about prompt engineering, How the model understands your question?, How the prompt is being processed to generate the response, and more importantly, even after the same prompt, why the response differ?
And if you can answer the above question, congrats once again, now you have everything needed to know about prompt engineering.
To become a successful prompt engineer, it’s very important to understand how the model acts on any given prompt and how a good prompt engineer make model perform at its best.
Thanks for reading this.
